steps.title: Identify what the underlined portion does
steps.content: Camillo says his presence might be “some allay” to the king’s sorrows, then immediately adds “or I o’erween [presume] to think so.” That parenthetical pulls back the claim — he’s acknowledging he merely assumes he can help, not that he knows it.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘establishes Camillo’s hope that Polixenes will be comforted knowing Camillo isn’t departing at his own discretion’: The underlined portion is about Camillo’s uncertainty over helping the Sicilian king, not about reassuring Polixenes. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘suggests Camillo feels compelled to persuade Polixenes that his decision to leave is justified’: Camillo isn’t building a persuasive case; he’s simply noting a hope and then qualifying it. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘conveys Camillo’s recognition that one benefit his presence may afford the king of Sicily is merely speculative’: Exactly — “I presume to think so” flags the benefit as an assumption, not a certainty. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘bolsters the idea that Camillo’s primary motivation is his concern for the king’s well-being’: The underlined portion actually weakens rather than bolsters the claim about the king’s well-being by marking it as presumed. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
